title: Diabetes: Overview and Management
content:
Diabetes is a disease that occurs when your blood glucose, also called blood sugar, is too high. Blood glucose is your main source of energy and comes from the food you eat. Insulin, a hormone made by the pancreas, helps glucose from food get into your cells to be used for energy. Sometimes your body doesn't make enough — or any — insulin or doesn't use insulin well. Glucose then stays in your blood and doesn't reach your cells.

Over time, having too much glucose in your blood can cause health problems. Although diabetes has no cure, you can take steps to manage your diabetes and stay healthy.

Type 2 diabetes is the most common form of diabetes. In type 2 diabetes, your body doesn't use insulin properly. This is called insulin resistance. At first, the pancreas makes extra insulin to make up for it. But, over time it can't keep up. Treatment includes lifestyle changes such as eating healthy foods, being active, and managing weight. You may also need diabetes medicines or insulin therapy.

Type 1 diabetes occurs when the immune system attacks and destroys the insulin-producing beta cells of the pancreas. People with Type 1 diabetes must take insulin every day. Common symptoms include increased thirst, frequent urination, extreme hunger, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue.

Managing diabetes includes monitoring blood sugar levels regularly, taking prescribed medications, eating a balanced diet, exercising regularly, and keeping healthcare appointments. Regular screening for complications such as eye disease, kidney disease, and nerve damage is important.

title: High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
content:
Blood pressure is the force of blood pushing against the walls of your arteries. Arteries carry blood from your heart to other parts of your body. Your blood pressure normally rises and falls throughout the day. If blood pressure stays high for a long time, it can damage your heart and lead to health problems including heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease.

A normal blood pressure reading is less than 120/80 mmHg. Elevated blood pressure is 120-129 systolic and less than 80 diastolic. Stage 1 hypertension is 130-139 systolic or 80-89 diastolic. Stage 2 hypertension is 140 or higher systolic or 90 or higher diastolic.

Lifestyle changes can help manage blood pressure: eat a healthy diet low in salt (the DASH eating plan), maintain a healthy weight, be physically active for at least 150 minutes per week, limit alcohol, manage stress, and quit smoking. If lifestyle changes alone are not enough, your doctor may prescribe blood pressure medications such as diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, or beta-blockers.

High blood pressure usually has no warning signs or symptoms. The only way to know if you have high blood pressure is to measure it. Regular check-ups are important.

title: Understanding Your Medications: Common Drug Classes
content:
Medications work in different ways to treat disease. Understanding how your medications work can help you use them safely and effectively.

Antibiotics fight bacterial infections. They work by killing bacteria or preventing them from reproducing. Common types include penicillins (amoxicillin), cephalosporins (cephalexin), macrolides (azithromycin), and fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin). Antibiotics do not work against viruses such as colds or flu. Taking antibiotics when they are not needed can lead to antibiotic resistance.

Pain medications include over-the-counter options like acetaminophen (Tylenol) and NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), as well as prescription opioids (oxycodone, hydrocodone) for severe pain. NSAIDs reduce inflammation and pain but can cause stomach upset and kidney problems with long-term use. Opioids carry risks of dependence and should be used only as directed.

Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, simvastatin) lower cholesterol by blocking a substance your body needs to make cholesterol. They reduce the risk of heart attack and stroke. Common side effects include muscle pain and elevated liver enzymes.

Blood thinners (anticoagulants) such as warfarin, apixaban, and rivaroxaban help prevent blood clots. They are prescribed for conditions like atrial fibrillation, deep vein thrombosis, and after certain surgeries. Regular monitoring may be needed, especially with warfarin.

Always take medications as prescribed, inform your healthcare provider about all medications you are taking (including supplements), and report any side effects promptly.

title: Heart Disease Prevention and Healthy Lifestyle
content:
Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the United States. The most common type is coronary artery disease, which can lead to heart attack. You can greatly reduce your risk through lifestyle changes and, if needed, medicine.

Heart-healthy eating includes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats (olive oil, nuts, fish). Limit saturated fats, trans fats, sodium, red meat, sweets, and sugar-sweetened beverages. The Mediterranean diet and DASH diet are both evidence-based approaches to reducing heart disease risk.

Regular physical activity strengthens your heart and improves circulation. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week, such as brisk walking, or 75 minutes of vigorous activity such as running. Include muscle-strengthening activities at least 2 days per week.

Maintain a healthy weight. Being overweight or obese increases your risk for heart disease. Even losing 5-10% of your body weight can improve your blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar levels.

Quit smoking. Smoking damages blood vessels, raises blood pressure, and reduces the amount of oxygen your blood can carry. Within 1 year of quitting, your risk of heart disease drops dramatically.

Manage stress through healthy activities like exercise, meditation, spending time with loved ones, or talking to a mental health professional. Chronic stress may contribute to heart disease risk factors.

title: Asthma: Symptoms, Triggers, and Management
content:
Asthma is a chronic lung disease that inflames and narrows the airways. It causes recurring periods of wheezing, chest tightness, shortness of breath, and coughing. Coughing often occurs at night or early in the morning.

Asthma affects people of all ages, but it most often starts during childhood. In the United States, more than 25 million people are known to have asthma.

Common asthma triggers include allergens (dust mites, pet dander, pollen, mold), irritants (cigarette smoke, air pollution, strong odors, chemical fumes), respiratory infections, exercise, cold air, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), emotional stress, and certain medications (aspirin, beta-blockers).

Asthma is managed with two types of medicines: long-term control medicines (taken daily to reduce inflammation and prevent symptoms) and quick-relief or rescue medicines (taken during asthma attacks for fast relief). Inhaled corticosteroids are the most effective long-term control medication. Short-acting beta-agonists like albuterol are the most common quick-relief medicines.

An asthma action plan is a written plan that you develop with your doctor to help control your asthma. It includes your daily treatment (medications and doses), how to handle worsening asthma, and when to seek emergency care.

Monitor your asthma with a peak flow meter, which measures how well air moves out of your lungs. Know your personal best peak flow number and use it to track your asthma control.

title: Mental Health: Depression and Anxiety
content:
Depression and anxiety disorders are among the most common mental health conditions. They can affect anyone, and they are not a sign of weakness.

Depression (major depressive disorder) causes a persistent feeling of sadness and loss of interest. Symptoms include changes in sleep and appetite, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, feelings of worthlessness or guilt, and thoughts of death or suicide. Depression is more than just feeling sad — it's a medical condition that affects how you think, feel, and handle daily activities.

Anxiety disorders involve more than temporary worry or fear. For people with anxiety disorders, the anxiety does not go away and can get worse over time. Types include generalized anxiety disorder (GAD), panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, and specific phobias. Symptoms may include restlessness, rapid heartbeat, trouble concentrating, excessive worrying, sleep problems, and avoiding certain situations.

Treatment options include psychotherapy (talk therapy), especially cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), which helps you identify and change negative thought patterns. Medications such as antidepressants (SSRIs like sertraline or fluoxetine, SNRIs like venlafaxine) and anti-anxiety medications may be prescribed. A combination of therapy and medication is often the most effective approach.

Self-care strategies include regular exercise, adequate sleep, healthy eating, staying connected with supportive people, limiting alcohol and avoiding drugs, and practicing relaxation techniques. If you or someone you know is in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.

title: Vaccination: What You Need to Know
content:
Vaccines are one of the most effective ways to prevent infectious diseases. They work by training your immune system to recognize and fight specific pathogens (viruses or bacteria) without causing the disease.

How vaccines work: Vaccines contain weakened, killed, or parts of the germ that causes a disease. When you get vaccinated, your immune system responds by producing antibodies and memory cells. If you are later exposed to the actual disease, your immune system can quickly recognize and fight it.

Recommended adult vaccines include: annual influenza (flu) vaccine, COVID-19 vaccine and boosters (as recommended), Tdap/Td (tetanus, diphtheria, pertussis) booster every 10 years, shingles vaccine (Shingrix, for adults 50+), pneumococcal vaccine (for adults 65+ or with certain health conditions), hepatitis B vaccine (if not previously vaccinated), and HPV vaccine (through age 26, or shared decision through age 45).

Vaccine safety: Vaccines undergo rigorous testing through clinical trials before approval and continue to be monitored after they are in use. Side effects are usually mild (soreness at injection site, low-grade fever, fatigue) and resolve within a few days. Serious side effects are very rare.

Herd immunity occurs when a large portion of a community becomes immune to a disease, making its spread unlikely. This indirectly protects people who cannot be vaccinated, such as infants, pregnant women, or people with weakened immune systems.

Keep your vaccination records up to date and discuss your immunization needs with your healthcare provider, especially if you have chronic health conditions, are pregnant, or plan to travel internationally.

title: Back Pain: Causes, Treatment, and Prevention
content:
Back pain is one of the most common reasons people see a doctor or miss work. Most back pain gets better within a few weeks with self-care.

Common causes include muscle or ligament strain (from heavy lifting or awkward movements), bulging or ruptured discs, arthritis (especially osteoarthritis), skeletal irregularities (scoliosis), and osteoporosis. Risk factors include age, lack of exercise, excess weight, diseases (some types of arthritis and cancer), improper lifting, psychological conditions, and smoking.

When to see a doctor: Most back pain gradually improves with home treatment and self-care. However, seek medical attention if back pain is severe and doesn't improve with rest, spreads down one or both legs (especially below the knee), causes weakness, numbness, or tingling in one or both legs, is accompanied by unexplained weight loss, or occurs with fever.

Treatment options include over-the-counter pain relievers (NSAIDs, acetaminophen), muscle relaxants, physical therapy (exercises to increase flexibility, strengthen back and abdominal muscles, and improve posture), heat and ice therapy, and in some cases, surgery.

Prevention strategies: Exercise regularly — low-impact activities that don't strain your back can increase strength and endurance. Build muscle strength and flexibility through core exercises, stretching, and Pilates or yoga. Maintain a healthy weight. Practice good posture and use proper body mechanics when lifting: bend at your knees, keep the load close to your body, and avoid twisting.

title: Healthy Sleep Habits
content:
Good sleep is essential for physical and mental health. Adults need 7-9 hours of sleep per night. Poor sleep quality or insufficient sleep is linked to obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, depression, and impaired immune function.

Sleep hygiene tips: Keep a consistent sleep schedule — go to bed and wake up at the same time every day, including weekends. Create a comfortable sleep environment — keep your room dark, quiet, and cool (65-68°F). Remove electronic devices from the bedroom or stop using them at least 30 minutes before bedtime.

Avoid stimulants close to bedtime: caffeine can stay in your system for 6-8 hours, so avoid coffee, tea, and chocolate in the afternoon and evening. Avoid large meals, alcohol, and nicotine before bedtime. While alcohol may help you fall asleep, it disrupts your sleep later in the night.

Common sleep disorders include insomnia (difficulty falling or staying asleep), sleep apnea (breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep), restless legs syndrome (uncomfortable sensations in the legs with an urge to move them), and narcolepsy (excessive daytime sleepiness). If you consistently have trouble sleeping, talk to your healthcare provider.

Regular physical activity can help you fall asleep faster and enjoy deeper sleep. However, avoid vigorous exercise close to bedtime. Stress management through relaxation techniques, meditation, or deep breathing exercises can also improve sleep quality.

title: Allergies: Types, Symptoms, and Treatment
content:
An allergy is a reaction by your immune system to something that does not bother most other people. Substances that cause allergic reactions are called allergens. Common allergens include pollen, dust mites, pet dander, mold, insect stings, certain foods (peanuts, tree nuts, milk, eggs, wheat, soy, fish, shellfish), and certain medications (penicillin, aspirin).

Allergic reactions can range from mild (sneezing, runny nose, itchy eyes) to severe (anaphylaxis, a life-threatening emergency). Anaphylaxis symptoms include difficulty breathing, swelling of the throat, rapid pulse, dizziness, and a severe drop in blood pressure. If you experience anaphylaxis, use an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately and call 911.

Diagnosis involves a medical history review, physical exam, skin prick tests, blood tests (specific IgE), and in some cases, oral food challenges. Treatment options include avoiding known allergens, antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, diphenhydramine) for mild symptoms, nasal corticosteroid sprays for allergic rhinitis, decongestants for short-term relief, and immunotherapy (allergy shots or sublingual tablets) for long-term desensitization.

Food allergies affect approximately 8% of children and 10% of adults. There is currently no cure for food allergies. Strict avoidance of the allergen and carrying an epinephrine auto-injector are essential management strategies. Always read food labels carefully and inform restaurants about your allergies.

title: Nutrition and Healthy Eating
content:
Good nutrition is one of the keys to a healthy life. You can improve your health by keeping a balanced diet. You should eat foods that contain vitamins and minerals, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, dairy, and a source of protein.

Key nutrients your body needs include: Carbohydrates (main source of energy — choose whole grains, fruits, and vegetables over refined sugars), Proteins (essential for building and repairing tissues — sources include lean meats, poultry, fish, beans, nuts, and dairy), Fats (necessary for nutrient absorption and brain function — choose unsaturated fats from olive oil, nuts, avocado, and fish), Vitamins and Minerals (support immune function, bone health, and many body processes), Fiber (aids digestion and helps maintain healthy weight — found in whole grains, fruits, vegetables, and legumes), and Water (essential for every body function — aim for 8 cups per day or more).

The Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommend: Fill half your plate with fruits and vegetables. Make at least half your grains whole grains. Choose lean proteins. Limit foods high in added sugars, saturated fat, and sodium. Manage portion sizes.

Reading nutrition labels helps you make informed food choices. Pay attention to serving size, calories, and amounts of key nutrients. Compare similar products to choose healthier options.

Special dietary considerations may apply for people with conditions such as diabetes, heart disease, kidney disease, food allergies, or during pregnancy. Consult with a registered dietitian for personalized nutrition advice.

title: Kidney Disease: What You Should Know
content:
Your kidneys are two bean-shaped organs, each about the size of a fist. They are located just below the rib cage, one on each side of your spine. Every day, your two kidneys filter about 150 quarts of blood to produce about 1 to 2 quarts of urine.

Chronic kidney disease (CKD) means your kidneys are damaged and can't filter blood the way they should. The main risk factors are diabetes and high blood pressure, which cause about 2 out of 3 cases. Other risk factors include heart disease, family history of kidney disease, and being over age 60.

CKD often has no symptoms in its early stages. As it gets worse, you may notice swelling in your feet and ankles, fatigue, trouble concentrating, decreased appetite, muscle cramps, puffy eyes (especially in the morning), dry and itchy skin, and frequent urination (especially at night).

Diagnosis involves blood tests (serum creatinine to calculate GFR) and urine tests (to check for albumin). A GFR below 60 for three or more months, or kidney damage indicated by albumin in the urine, indicates CKD.

Management includes controlling blood pressure (goal typically <130/80 mmHg), managing blood sugar if you have diabetes, eating a kidney-friendly diet (limiting sodium, potassium, and phosphorus as kidney function declines), staying physically active, maintaining a healthy weight, and not smoking. Certain medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors) can help protect the kidneys. If kidneys fail, dialysis or a kidney transplant is needed to survive.

title: Exercise and Physical Fitness
content:
Regular physical activity is one of the most important things you can do for your health. It can reduce your risk of chronic diseases, improve your mental health and mood, strengthen your bones and muscles, and help you maintain a healthy weight.

Physical activity guidelines for adults: At least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity (like brisk walking) or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity (like running) per week. Muscle-strengthening activities that work all major muscle groups at least 2 days per week. Reduce sedentary time — even small amounts of physical activity are beneficial.

Types of exercise: Aerobic (cardio) exercises include walking, jogging, cycling, swimming, and dancing. Strength training includes weightlifting, resistance bands, bodyweight exercises, and Pilates. Flexibility exercises include stretching and yoga. Balance exercises (important for older adults) include tai chi and standing on one foot.

Exercise benefits for specific conditions: For heart disease — improves cardiovascular fitness and reduces blood pressure. For diabetes — improves insulin sensitivity and blood sugar control. For depression and anxiety — releases endorphins and improves mood. For arthritis — reduces joint pain and improves function. For osteoporosis — weight-bearing exercises build bone strength.

Starting an exercise program: Begin slowly and gradually increase duration and intensity. Choose activities you enjoy. Set realistic goals. Exercise with a friend for motivation. If you have a chronic condition, talk to your doctor before starting a new exercise program.

title: Understanding Lab Tests and Results
content:
Lab tests are medical procedures that involve testing a sample of your blood, urine, or other body tissue. They can help find a condition before you have symptoms, confirm or rule out a diagnosis, plan or evaluate treatment, and monitor a condition over time.

Common blood tests include: Complete Blood Count (CBC) measures white blood cells (fight infection), red blood cells (carry oxygen), hemoglobin, hematocrit, and platelets. Abnormal values could indicate infection, anemia, clotting problems, blood cancers, or immune system disorders.

Basic Metabolic Panel (BMP) measures glucose, calcium, sodium, potassium, bicarbonate, chloride, BUN, and creatinine. It helps evaluate kidney function, blood sugar levels, and electrolyte balance.

Lipid Panel measures total cholesterol, LDL (bad cholesterol, goal <100 mg/dL for high-risk), HDL (good cholesterol, goal >40 mg/dL men, >50 mg/dL women), and triglycerides (goal <150 mg/dL). Used to assess cardiovascular disease risk.

Hemoglobin A1C (HbA1c) reflects average blood sugar levels over 2-3 months. Normal: <5.7%, Prediabetes: 5.7-6.4%, Diabetes: ≥6.5%. Used for both diagnosis and monitoring of diabetes.

Thyroid Function Tests measure TSH, Free T4, and Free T3 to evaluate thyroid function. Elevated TSH suggests hypothyroidism; suppressed TSH suggests hyperthyroidism.

Liver Function Tests (ALT, AST, ALP, bilirubin, albumin) help detect liver damage or disease. Elevated transaminases may indicate hepatitis, fatty liver disease, or medication toxicity.

Understanding your results: Results are compared to reference ranges. Abnormal results don't always mean you have a medical condition — discuss results with your healthcare provider for proper interpretation.

title: First Aid Basics
content:
Knowing basic first aid can help you handle injuries and emergencies before professional medical help arrives.

For bleeding: Apply firm pressure with a clean cloth or bandage. Keep the injured area elevated above heart level if possible. Apply pressure continuously for at least 15 minutes. If blood soaks through, add more material on top — do not remove the original bandage. Seek emergency care for severe or uncontrollable bleeding.

For burns: Cool the burn under cool (not cold) running water for at least 10-20 minutes. Do not apply ice, butter, or toothpaste. Cover with a sterile, non-stick bandage. Take over-the-counter pain relievers if needed. Seek medical attention for large burns, burns on the face/hands/feet/joints, deep burns, or chemical and electrical burns.

For choking (Heimlich maneuver): Stand behind the person. Place your fist just above the navel. Grasp your fist with your other hand. Give quick, upward abdominal thrusts until the object is expelled. Call 911 if the person becomes unconscious.

For heart attack symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, pain in arm/jaw/back, nausea, cold sweat): Call 911 immediately. Have the person chew an aspirin (unless allergic). Begin CPR if the person becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally.

CPR basics: Call 911. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest at a rate of 100-120 compressions per minute. Allow full chest recoil between compressions. If trained, give rescue breaths (30 compressions to 2 breaths). Use an AED if available — turn it on and follow the voice prompts.
